coredata.dc:title: Preparation of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles regulated by CoOOH nanosheets for detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qing
Guo, Yu-Meng
Wu, Zhan
Li, Guang-Li
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important enzyme which is widely distributed in organisms and participate in the vital process of dephosphorylation, often serve as a common biomarker for clinical diagnosis and treatment of human diseases. Herein, we reported a novel and simple strategy for the preparation of polydopamine nanoparticles (PDANPs) by employing CoOOH nanosheets as an oxidant. In the presence of CoOOH nanosheets, dopamine is easy to be oxidized and self-polymerize into fluorescent PDANPs. By employing the synthesized PDANPs as an effective fluorescence indicator, a fluorescence biosensor was established for label-free and sensitive determination of ALP activity based on the redox reaction between CoOOH nanosheets and the ALP hydrolysis product ascorbic acid (AA), and the dependence of CoOOH nanosheets concentration for formation of PDANPs. In the presence of ALP, the substrate ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (Asc-2P) was catalytically hydrolyzed and released free ascorbic acid, which could in situ reduction of Co(III) of CoOOH to Co(II) ions, resulting in the suppression of oxidation ability of CoOOH nanosheets, thus preventing the synthesis of fluorescent PDA. Therefore, the activity of ALP can be feasibly detected according to the changes of fluorescence peak at 490 nm of PDANPs. The sensor displays a linear range from 0.5 U/L to 300 U/L with a detection limit of 0.1 U/L. Moreover, the developed strategy exhibits good selectivity over other potential interference existing in human serum sample. The synthesis process of fluorescence PDANPs was facile and cost-effective, the proposed convenient and label-free fluorescence PDANPs detection platform shows potential applications in clinical diagnosis and provides a new paradigm for biological analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticle
CoOOH nanosheets
Alkaline phosphatase
Label-free
Fluorescence assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ALP activity sensing based on the fluorescent PDANPs detection platform
Selectivity of fluorescent PDANPs detection platform for ALP activity sensing
Determination of ALP activity in human serum sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21705042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of the mixture of dopamine and CoOOH nanosheets after incubation for 60 min (a), dopamine (b) and CoOOH nanosheets (c). The inset is the corresponding digital photos. (B) TEM image of the prepared PDANPs. Scale bar: 50 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PDANPs in different systems. ALP + Asc-2P + Dopamine + CoOOH nanosheets (a), ALP + Dopamine + CoOOH nanosheets (b), Asc-2P + Dopamine + CoOOH nanosheets (c), Dopamine + CoOOH nanosheets (d). Reaction was performed at 75 μg/mL CoOOH nanosheets, 250 μM dopamine, 50 μg/mL Asc-2P, 300 U/L ALP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PDANPs in response to ALP of various concentrations. (B) The corresponding fluorescence response at 490 nm. Inset: a linear plot of fluorescence intensities against ALP concentrations.

Selectivity for ALP assay using the fluorescence PDA detection platform. The amount of these enzymes and proteins were 1 mg/mL, the concentration for small molecular and metal ions is 100 μM, the concentration for ALP and NEM are 200 U/L and 500 μM, respectively. (F0 and F is the fluorescence intensity value at 490 nm in the absence and presence of ALP enzyme).
Fluorescent PDANPs for determination of the activity of alkaline phosphatase.

head.ce:title: Preparation of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles regulated by CoOOH nanosheets for detection of alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Biomedical Nanomaterials and Devices, College of Life Science and Chemistry, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou, 412007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Biomedical Nanomaterials and Devices
College of Life Science and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Hunan Key Laboratory of Biomedical Nanomaterials and Devices, College of Life Science and Chemistry, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou, 412007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Bio-Sensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Bio-Sensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Bio-Sensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important enzyme which is widely distributed in organisms and participate in the vital process of dephosphorylation, often serve as a common biomarker for clinical diagnosis and treatment of human diseases. Herein, we reported a novel and simple strategy for the preparation of polydopamine nanoparticles (PDANPs) by employing CoOOH nanosheets as an oxidant. In the presence of CoOOH nanosheets, dopamine is easy to be oxidized and self-polymerize into fluorescent PDANPs. By employing the synthesized PDANPs as an effective fluorescence indicator, a fluorescence biosensor was established for label-free and sensitive determination of ALP activity based on the redox reaction between CoOOH nanosheets and the ALP hydrolysis product ascorbic acid (AA), and the dependence of CoOOH nanosheets concentration for formation of PDANPs. In the presence of ALP, the substrate ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (Asc-2P) was catalytically hydrolyzed and released free ascorbic acid, which could in situ reduction of Co(III) of CoOOH to Co(II) ions, resulting in the suppression of oxidation ability of CoOOH nanosheets, thus preventing the synthesis of fluorescent PDA. Therefore, the activity of ALP can be feasibly detected according to the changes of fluorescence peak at 490 nm of PDANPs. The sensor displays a linear range from 0.5 U/L to 300 U/L with a detection limit of 0.1 U/L. Moreover, the developed strategy exhibits good selectivity over other potential interference existing in human serum sample. The synthesis process of fluorescence PDANPs was facile and cost-effective, the proposed convenient and label-free fluorescence PDANPs detection platform shows potential applications in clinical diagnosis and provides a new paradigm for biological analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a vital and indispensable protease in the process of dephosphorylation of variety of substrate including protein, nucleic acid or small molecule [1,2]. This function plays essential roles in intracellular protein activity regulation and signaling pathway. Currently, alkaline phosphatase has been proved to be an important biomarker for many diseases, such as liver disease [3], senile dementia [4], obstruction of biliary tract [5] and prostate cancer with bone metastases [6]. Hence, the development of a sensitive strategies for the detection of ALP is significant in the study the mechanism of the process of phosphate hydrolysis, clinical diagnosis and medical research. To this end, a variety of analytical approach and technologies include colorimetric [7,8], fluorescence [9,10], electrochemistry [11,12], surface enhancement Raman scattering [13,14] have been established for detection of alkaline phosphatase. Among these techniques, fluorescence biosensors hold significant advantages in analysis speed, sensitivity, selectivity and simple operation. Furthermore, the development of nanotechnology has greatly promoted and fueled the possibility of employing nanomaterials as a label-free fluorescence indicator for fluorescent biosensor, many workers have been dedicated to designing nanomaterial-based biosensors with convenient, sensitive and reliable [15–19].

So far, fluorescent nanomaterials such as noble metal nanoclusters [20], transition metal semiconductor quantum dots [21], coordination polymer [22] have been reported for determination of ALP activity. Despite the great accomplishment that have been acquired, the above performance of these nanomaterials still suffers from some shortcomings, such as the inevitable toxicity issue of quantum dots, expensive and less stability of nanometer sized metal clusters, time-consuming and labor intensive of coordination polymer, which limit their further application. Therefore, finding suitable fluorescent nanomaterials to sensitively and selectively monitor the ALP content is still of great value, in particular, the exploitation of convenient and ease of synthesis, low cost and good photostability of fluorescent nanomaterials with non-toxicity, cost-effective.

Recently, mounting attentions have been paid to fluorescence polymer nanoparticles because of their remarkable stability, biocompatibility and ease of surface functionalization [23]. Dopamine, as the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain, which can regulate many physiological functions of the central nervous system [24]. In the condition of alkaline environment or oxidative stress, catechol of dopamine can be oxidized to quinone derivatives, and then auto polymerize into polydopamine nanoparticles [25]. With advantages such as excellent photostability and intense fluorescence, polydopamine nanoparticles (PDANPs) have become an extremely promising fluorescent agent in the field of biosensor [26–31]. Tang's group reported the synthesis of PDANPs using MnO2 nanosheets as oxidant and determination of alpha fetoprotein by dissolution of MnO2 nanoparticles with GSH [26]. Zheng's group adopt ficin as a peroxidase-mimic synthesized intrinsic fluorescent PDANPs and further applied for rapid dopamine sensing [27]. Recently, Tian et al. developed alkaline phosphatase (ALP) detection platform based on the quenching effect of Hg2+ towards fluorescent PDANPs and the redox reaction between Hg2+ and ascorbic acid. Nevertheless, other coexisting ions such as Fe2+ and Cu2+ strongly influence the detection results, also the reduction products Hg0 has high biological toxicity [28]. To date, there have been several strategies include oxidation, conjugation, carbonization and degradation to fabricated fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles (PDANPs) [25]. These synthesis methods have some disadvantages such as time consuming (even several days) and complex operation. Therefore, to explore a rapid and facile synthetic method of fluorescence PDANPs and construct more reliable and innovative detection system for bioanalysis is still promising.

Cobalt hydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets are two-dimensional nanomaterials, which can be synthesized rapidly and show good stability, large surface area and broad light absorption [32–34] These properties enable CoOOH nanosheets to be a promising nanomaterial in the field of biosensor and bioanalysis. On the one hand, CoOOH nanosheets shows a wide UV absorption range and can quench the fluorescence of most fluorescent dyes or fluorescent nanoparticles, so they often used as an efficient nanoquencher for fluorophore groups [32]. On the other hand, CoOOH nanosheets are also a kind of oxidant regents, which can react with reducing substances and convert to Co(II) ions. This feature is often used in detection and analysis of reductive biomolecules [34]. However, the development of the oxidation capacity of CoOOH nanosheets to synthesize new nanomaterials and then further constructing biosensor detection systems is rarely reported.

Herein, we present a strategy for fabrication of intrinsic fluorescent PDANPs with cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets as an oxidant, and further developed a novel platform for sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity. Scheme 1 outlines the design principle of the CoOOH nanosheets induced formation of fluorescent PDANPs for ALP detection assay. As a transition metal oxyhydroxide, CoOOH nanosheets has the ability of oxidation and can be decomposed into Co(II) ions by reducing agents. Therefore, dopamine could be oxidized into its quinone derivative under the oxidation of CoOOH nanosheets, then auto polymerized to form PDANPs with strong fluorescence. The amount of CoOOH nanosheets plays a key role in the synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs. As reducing agent, ascorbic acid could reduce Co(III) of CoOOH nanosheets to Co(II) ions. Therefore, we consider that this redox reaction could consume the oxidation agent for further formation of PDANPs, leading a decreased fluorescence intensity of PDANPs. Inspired by the fact that the specific substrate ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (Asc-2P) can be catalyzed by alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and release free ascorbic acid, we speculated that the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) could in situ regulate the synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs by the redox reaction between AA and CoOOH nanosheets. Therefore, the activity of ALP was facilely detected through the change of the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs. Hence, the proposed strategy can offer a new insight on the synthesis of PDANPs and develop a label-free and convenient tool for determination of ALP activity.

Alkaline phosphatase from bovine intestinal mucosa and ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (Asc-2P) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Sodium hydroxide, cobalt chloride (CoCl2), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO·5H2O) and ascorbic acid were purchased from Alfa Assar chemical co., LTD. Ultrapure water was acquired by Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, MA) with electric resistance >18.2 MΩ. Human plasma samples were obtained from Xiangya Medical College, Central South University (Changsha, China).

The fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature on an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh instruments Ltd). The fluorescence spectra of PDANPs were recorded from 420 to 640 nm with an excitation wavelength at 400 nm. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was carried out on a Shimadzu-2600 UV–visible spectroscopy. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a Hitachi H-7000 electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

According to previous reports [35], the synthesis process of CoOOH nanosheets are as follows: firstly, 500 μL 10 mM of cobalt chloride solution and 125 μL sodium hydroxide solution (1.0 M) was mixed evenly and sonicated for 1 min. After that, 25 μL of 0.9 M sodium hypochlorite solution was reacted the above reaction system, and ultrasound at 25 °C for 30 min. The obtained product was centrifuged at 16000 rpm for 10 min under 4 °C, then washed 4 times with water. Finally, the synthesized CoOOH nanosheets were dispersed in 1 ml of water, and then placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for use.

To synthesize fluorescent PDANPs, 75 μL sterilized water, 10 μL PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) buffer, and 10 μL CoOOH nanosheets (0.75 mg/mL) were mixed. After that, 5 μL 50 mM fresh-prepared dopamine solution was added to the above mixture and incubated for 1 h to synthesis fluorescent PDA. Finally, 6 μL 0.2 M hydrochloric acid (12 m M) were added to the above mixture to stop the polymerization reaction.

Various concentrations of AA solution were incubated with the aqueous solution containing10 μL PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) buffer, and 10 μL CoOOH nanosheets (0.75 mg/mL), then incubated at room temperature for 10 min to ensure sufficient reaction. After that, 5 μL 50 mM dopamine was added to the mixed solution and incubated under the same conditions for 1 h. Finally, 6 μL 0.2 M hydrochloric acid solution was added to the above reaction system and cultured for 3 min then the fluorescence spectra were measured.

For ALP activity detection, 10 μL 0.75 mg/mL CoOOH nanosheets, 50 μg/mL Asc-2P, and various amounts of ALP were reacted with the above system contain PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) buffer at 37 °C for 30 min. Then 5 μL 50 mM dopamine was added and incubated at room temperature for 1 h. Finally, 6 μL 0.2 M HCl was added to the solution to terminate the reaction. After that, all samples were excited at 400 nm, and fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 420–640 nm. The excitation and emission slits were set to 5 nm with a 950 V PMT voltage.

For serum samples, trichloroacetic acid (15%) was added in advance to precipitate the protein in serum, and the mixture was incubated on a shaker for 15 min, then centrifuged at 10000 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min, and the collected supernatant was adjusted to pH 7.4. After that, N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) was added into the above diluted human serum solution to clean up sulfhydryl in serum. Then various concentrations of ALP were added into the pretreated serum to obtain spiked sample. The following ALP spiked sample for recovery tests were similar to the preceding methods.

The morphology of CoOOH nanosheets was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As we can see in Fig. S1, the obtained CoOOH nanosheets presented a thin and hexagonal shape, which is consistent with literature reports.37 Meanwhile, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of CoOOH nanosheets showed a wide band in the range of 300–800 nm, and the maximum absorption peak is ~410 nm (Fig. S2). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern further verified the growth of CoOOH phase (JCPDS 07–0169) on the cobalt substrate (JCPDS 05–0727). The positions and the relative intensity ratios of the diffraction peaks closely matched with reported results of CoOOH (Fig. S3) [36]. The above results showed that CoOOH nanosheets have been successfully prepared.

Fig. 1A demonstrated that the fluorescent PDANPs were successfully synthesized. After incubation of CoOOH nanosheets with dopamine, we can observe a strong characteristic fluorescence peak at 490 nm (curve a). However, we can hardly see fluorescence signal when the system contained only dopamine (curve b) or CoOOH nanosheets (curve c). The inset photos also displayed that the color changes of dopamine after the incubation of CoOOH nanosheets. Once the CoOOH nanosheets added, the colorless dopamine solution turned into a light-yellow. These results indicated that dopamine was successfully changed into quinone derivative and polymerized to form fluorescent PDANPs under the oxidation of CoOOH nanosheets. We speculated that the chemical oxidation of dopamine by CoOOH nanosheets result in a high proportion of carbonyl and carboxyl groups in the NPs and weakened the fluorescence quenching caused by π-π stacking interaction between the structural units in PDA. According to TEM image in Fig. 1 B, PDANPs were sphere in shape and have a better dispersivity. Fluorescent PDANPs has a relatively wide particle size distribution, mainly with an average particle size of about 5 nm, which is as a result of the formation of particles with distinct dopamine oligomers and different oligomeric stacking. The results of component analysis show that fluorescent PDANPs contain C, N and O elements (Fig. S4). These results indicated that we successfully obtained fluorescent PDANPs.

The fluorescence properties of the prepared PDANPs were further investigated, as shown in Fig. S5, the maximum excitation wavelength of the fluorescence intensities of PDANPs is 400 nm while the maximum emission peak is located at 490 nm. The fluorescence emission wavelength of PDANPs at 490 nm was nearly excitation-independent with the excitation wavelength in the range of 360–420 nm (Fig. S6), which is analogue to the synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs by hydrothermal method under acidic condition.25 We inferred that the reason may be the existence of the weak acidic dopamine solution in the reaction system. We further investigated the photostability of PDANPs as shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized fluorescent PDANPs has basically no change within seven days, indicating the fluorescent PDANPs have the advantage of the long-term photostability. Furthermore, the stability of PDANPs was also tested under different acidity conditions (Fig. S8). As a result, the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs scarcely exhibited significant change over the pH range from 3 to 12, suggesting the PDANPs have the potential in the field of biological analysis applications under physiological conditions.

In order to obtain PDANPs with the maximum fluorescence intensity, the influence of reaction conditions was examined including the concentration of CoOOH nanosheets and reaction time during the synthesis procedure. As observed from Fig. S9, 250 μM dopamine was incubation with various concentration of CoOOH nanosheets, with the increasing concentration of CoOOH nanosheets from 0 to 75 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of PDANPs increased gradually. However, it started to drop down upon further increasing the amount of CoOOH nanosheets. The reason for this phenomenon might be the fluorescence quenching effect caused by excessive CoOOH nanosheets and the generation of large size PDA particles. The results also further confirm that the CoOOH nanosheets oxidize dopamine to synthesize PDANPs. Hence, 75 μg/mL CoOOH nanosheets was choose as the optimal concentration while the concentration of dopamine was fixed at 250 μM. Based on the above optimization, the reaction time of dopamine oxidation by CoOOH nanosheets was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity increased with the extension of reaction time and reached the strongest value at 60 min. After 60 min, the fluorescence intensity nearly remained a stable value. Therefore, 60 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time. According to reports, strong acids can slow down the polymerization rate of dopamine or even terminate the polymerization reaction [37]. Therefore, after 60 min of reaction, we added different concentration of hydrochloric acid to the reaction system immediately to stop the polymerization, and studied the termination effect of different concentrations of hydrochloric acid. Fig. S11 shows the addition of hydrochloric acid slightly affected the fluorescence intensity of dopamine. From the experimental data, compared with no addition of hydrochloric acid, the intensity of PDANPs displayed a higher fluorescence peak value, that might due to the process of polymerization still continued before the fluorescence test. When the concentration of hydrochloric acid reached 6 mM, the fluorescence signal gradually stabilized. Therefore, we selected 6 mM hydrochloric acid as the termination agent of polymerization. Also, we can see from Fig. S12, the emission intensity at 490 nm did not change significantly with time after the addition of hydrochloric acid, verified the polymerization process of fluorescent PDANPs was terminated basically.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP activity sensing based on the fluorescent PDANPs detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the key role of CoOOH nanosheets in the synthesis process of fluorescent PDANPs, we further explore the usefulness of the fluorescent PDANPs in the field of biosensors, and develop a sensitive and convenient fluorescence strategy for determination of AA based on redox reaction regulated concentration of CoOOH nanosheets, and subsequently affect the formation of fluorescence PDANPs. We firstly examined the fluorescent response of PDANPs system for AA sensing to ensure the feasibility of proposed method. In Fig. S13, when the system contained CoOOH nanosheets and dopamine, the intense PDANPs fluorescence could be observed. Conversely, in the present of AA, CoOOH nanosheets and dopamine, there is no obvious PDANPs fluorescence signal obtained, suggesting the reduction of oxidation ability of CoOOH nanosheets due to the redox reaction with AA. In addition, we also performed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the various reaction systems. As can be seen from Fig. S14, compare with dopamine, when incubation dopamine with CoOOH nanosheets, the UV absorption spectrum of the system exhibit a new broad absorption over the range of 300–700 nm, which was attributed to the presence of dopamine oxidation products as well as its polymers, indicating the occurrence of dopamine polymerization. However, in the present of AA, dopamine and CoOOH nanosheets, the absorption peak in the 300–700 nm of dopamine oxidation products disappeared, and the absorption curve was almost the same as the system contain only dopamine, confirming CoOOH nanosheets were effectively reduced by AA and not enable further synthesis PDANPs. On the basis of above results, the fluorescent PDANPs may have potential applications for detection of AA. Fig. S15A shows the ﬂuorescence response of PDANPs detection platform in the present of various concentrations of AA. It can be seen that in the range of 0～150 μM, as the concentration of AA increases, the peak intensity gradually decreases. As shown in Fig. S15C, the relationship between peak intensity at 490 nm and AA concentration displays a good linear correlation in the concentration range of 1–100 μM. The detection limit (LOD) was measured to be a favorable 0.3 μM from the calibration curve at a signal to noise ratio of 3σ. These results indicated that the proposed sensor holds the potential as a highly sensitive AA detecting platform.

It is well known that ALP can specifically catalyze Asc-2P hydrolysis yield free AA products. Therefore, by using AA as an intermediate, the sensing platform can be further applied to the determination of ALP because of the active catalytic ability of ALP. On the basis of preliminary results, the effect of ALP on the preparation of fluorescent PDANPs was next investigated to verify the design principle. Fig. 2 depicts typical fluorescence spectral responses of the assay. When Asc-2P and ALP were absent, an intense fluorescence peak at 490 nm can be observed when excited at 400 nm (curve d), verifying that the PDANPs were successfully synthesized through the oxidation of dopamine by a high concentration of CoOOH nanosheets. Regardless of adding ALP or Asc-2P to the mixture of dopamine and CoOOH nanosheets, the fluorescence signal substantially retained, indicating that ALP or Asc-2P does not influence synthesis of PDANPs (curve b and c). Conversely, when ALP was incubated with the reaction system, no fluorescence signal was displayed (curve a). The above results demonstrated that the established strategy based on ALP regulates the synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs can be achieved.

The performance of the developed strategy for quantitative analysis of activity of ALP was further investigated. Different concentrations of ALP reacted with Asc-2P at 37 °C for 1 h, then dopamine and CoOOH nanosheets were added to the reaction system for further formation of fluorescent PDANPs. The analytical performance of the method was evaluated by recording the peak intensity at 490 nm after adding hydrochloric acid. As shown in Fig. 3 , increasing ALP concentrations from 0.5 U/L to 300 U/L is found to give gradual decrease in fluorescence peaks. The relationship between peak intensity at 490 nm and ALP concentration shows a good linear correlation in the concentration range of 0.5 U/L to 300 U/L. The calibration equation was F = -2214C + 314152, with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9962, where F is the fluorescence intensity and C is the concentration of ALP. The detection limit was estimated to be 0.1 U/L according to the 3σ rule. Such a wide dynamic range was also much better than Hg2+ modulated fluorescence intensity of PDANPs for ALP assay [26]. This result is also comparable or even superior to than former methods for determination of ALP activity (Table S1), confirming that the developed strategy realized a sensitive platform for detection of ALP activity. Furthermore, the sensing process of the developed strategy for ALP is simple, and the preparation of fluorescent PDANPs is convenient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of fluorescent PDANPs detection platform for ALP activity sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further applied the developed method to practical samples, we firstly choose some metal ions, enzyme, protein, small molecular and investigated the selectivity of the present strategy. As shown in Fig. 4 , compared with other substances, the proposed method has good selectivity for the detection of ALP (except AA, glutathione and sodium sulfide). The concentration of AA in serum is about 20–100 μM [38], which can be achieved by diluting the sample. As for the ALP analysis in cancer cells, the low concentration of AA will not affect the determination results. Glutathione and Cys can also reduce CoOOH nanosheets to Co2+, and inhibit the production of fluorescent PDA. However, N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) can effectively react with thiols and serve as the scavenger of GSH and Cys, which can effectively avoid the interference without affecting the detection of ALP. Therefore, the reductive interfering substances from the samples can be reduced and avoided by appropriate methods for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALP activity in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: We next evaluated the practicability of this method by measuring ALP activity in diluted serum (1%). Five serum samples with different concentrations of ALP added in advance were measured as shown in Table S2. The experimental data show that the recoveries of ALP are between 93.3% and 103.0%. These desirable results demonstrated that the developed method has the potential to be used in the analysis of real samples.

In conclusion, we have developed a novel method for synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs by employing CoOOH nanosheets as an oxidant of dopamine. Furthermore, we construct a simple and sensitive ALP activity detection platform based on ALP regulation of synthesis of fluorescent PDANPs. As a fluorescent indicator, the synthesis of PDANPs by using CoOOH nanosheets as an oxidation regent is simple operation and low cost. This label-free sensor displayed excellent response performance for quantification of ALP with a detection limit of 0.1 U/L. The approach also successfully applied to detect ALP activity in human real samples. In virtue of these merits, this simple and novel strategy provides a convenient implement for ALP-related basic research and disease diagnosis.

Qing Li: Conception and design of study, acquisition of data, analysis and/or interpretation of data, Drafting the manuscript, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content. Yu-Meng Guo: acquisition of data. Zhan Wu: acquisition of data. Guang-Li Li: Conception and design of study, analysis and/or interpretation of data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705042).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low serum levels of alkaline phosphatase of bone origin: a good marker of adynamic bone disease in haemodialysis patients
